agra_mughal_empire_c_1650_ce:
Agra, Mughal Empire (c. 1650 CE)

The carpets, handicrafts, zari and zardozi (embroidery work), marble and stone carving and inlay work.

Architecture & Cityscape:
The Agra Fort is a large 16th-century fortress of red sandstone located by the Yamuna River in Agra.
Large fortified citadels or palace complexes, such as the Agra Fort and the Red Fort in Delhi, were enclosed in massive walls reinforced by semi-circular towers and entered via monumental gateways.

Daily Life & Culture:
Agra falls under Braj region and its local language is Braj Bhakha.
Beyond the famed Petha, Agra's cuisine is a blend of Mughal richness and local flavours.
amritsar_sikh_empire_c_1830_ce:
Amritsar, Sikh Empire (c. 1830 CE)

Architecture & Cityscape:
Dharamsals were succeeded by gurdwaras that housed the community's sacred scripture compiled by Guru Arjan, the most notable of which is the Darbar Sahib in Amritsar.
The project to build the Darbar Sahib shrine at Amritsar was a rallying point for the early Sikh community of the time.

Daily Life & Culture:
Sikhism has forged a unique form of architecture which Bhatti describes as being "inspired by Guru Nanak’s creative mysticism" such that Sikh architecture "is a mute harbinger of holistic humanism based on pragmatic spirituality".
The "jewel in the crown" of the Sikh Style is the Harmandir Sahib.
badami_chalukya_dynasty_c_600_ce:
Badami, Chalukya Dynasty (c. 600 CE)

Daily Life & Culture:
Hindi Film Guru featuring Abisek Bachan was shot in Badami.
A part of the Tamil film Taj Mahal was shot in Badami, with a part of the song 'Adi Manja Kilange' shot at Agastya Teertha.
belur_hoysala_empire_c_1150_ce:
Belur, Hoysala Empire (c. 1150 CE)

Ruvari Malithamma built the Kesava Temple at Somanathapura and worked on forty other monuments, including the Amruteshwara temple at Amruthapura.

Architecture & Cityscape:
Large and small temples built during this era remain as examples of the Hoysala architectural style, including the Chennakesava Temple at Belur, the Hoysaleswara Temple at Halebidu, and the Kesava Temple at Somanathapura.
This can be seen in the famous Chennakesava Temple at Belur dedicated to Vishnu and in the Hoysaleswara temple at Halebidu dedicated to Shiva.

delhi_delhi_sultanate_c_1350_ce:
Delhi, Delhi Sultanate (c. 1350 CE)

Economy & Trade:
As with other regions of India, the IT industry has been expanding in Delhi.

Architecture & Cityscape:
The architecture of Delhi dates back more than a thousand years.
After Delhi was declared the site for a new capital of India, George V laid the foundation of New Delhi, which would serve as the capital.

Daily Life & Culture:
Other monuments include the India Gate, the Jantar Mantar—an 18th-century astronomical observatory—and the Purana Qila—a 16th-century fortress.
Over the centuries, Delhi has become known for its composite culture, and a festival that symbolises this is the Phool Walon Ki Sair, which takes place in September.
delhi_indian_independence_1947:
Delhi, Indian Independence (1947)

Economy & Trade:
Delhi is the largest commercial center in northern India.
Delhi's large consumer market and the availability of skilled labour has also attracted foreign investment.

Daily Life & Culture:
Three World Heritage Sites—the Red Fort, Qutub Minar and Humayun's Tomb—are located in Delhi.
New Delhi houses several government buildings and official residences reminiscent of British colonial architecture, including the Rashtrapati Bhavan, the Secretariat, Rajpath, the Parliament of India and Vijay Chowk.
dholavira_indus_valley_civilization_c_25:
Dholavira, Indus Valley Civilization (c. 2500 BCE)

Political & Security:
Some of the seals found at Dholavira, belonging to Stage III, contained animal only figures, without any type of script.
It is suggested that these types of seals represent early conventions of Indus seal making.

Architecture & Cityscape:
Estimated to be older than the port-city of Lothal, the city of Dholavira has a rectangular shape and organization, and is spread over 22 ha (54 acres).
Dholavira is flanked by two storm water channels; the Mansar in the north, and the Manhar in the south.
ellora_rashtrakuta_dynasty_c_800_ce:
Ellora, Rashtrakuta Dynasty (c. 800 CE)

Architecture & Cityscape:
Cave 5 is unique among the Ellora caves as it was designed as a hall with a pair of parallel refectory benches in the centre and a Buddha statue in the rear.
The carvings in this cave are larger than life size but, according to author Dhavalikar, they are "corpulent, stumpy with disproportionate limbs" compared to those found in other Ellora caves.

Daily Life & Culture:
In the novel, Feluda travels to the Ellora caves to uncover a smuggling racket involving illegal trade of historical artifacts from Indian temples, including the largest cave at Ellora, the Kailasa Temple.
fatehpur_sikri_mughal_empire_c_1575_ce:
Fatehpur Sikri, Mughal Empire (c. 1575 CE)

Architecture & Cityscape:

Daily Life & Culture:
Vita Sackville-West, in her novel All Passion Spent, places the key meeting between Deborah, Lady Slane, and Mr FitzGeorge, at Fatehpur Sikri.
Salman Rushdie's novel The Enchantress of Florence is partly set in 16th century Fatehpur Sikri.
hampi_vijayanagara_empire_c_1500_ce:
Hampi, Vijayanagara Empire (c. 1500 CE)

Economy & Trade:
South of the Krishna temple's exterior are two adjacent shrines, one containing the largest monolithic Shiva Linga and the other with the largest monolithic Yoga-Narasimha avatar of Vishnu in Hampi.
The Vitthala temple has a Garuda shrine in the form of a stone chariot in the courtyard; it is an often-pictured symbol of Hampi.

Architecture & Cityscape:
The Hazara Rama temple, referred to as the Ramachandra temple in inscriptions, occupied the western part of the urban core in the royal centre section of Hampi.
The Hampi site includes a Muslim quarter with Islamic tombs, two mosques and a cemetery.
hastinapur_late_vedic_period_c_900_bce:
Hastinapur, Late Vedic Period (c. 900 BCE)

Political & Security:
Situated in the historic location of the ancient city of Hastinapur, the Pandeshwar temple is dedicated to Shiva.
This is a small Gurdwara located in the village of Saifpur, around 2.5 km (1.6 mi) from Hastinapur.

Architecture & Cityscape:
The Karna Temple is located near the Pandeshwar temple on an old ravine along the bank of the Ganges.
The Shivling inside the Karna Temple is believed to be established by Karna, one of the prominent figures in the Mahabharata.
kolkata_british_east_india_company_c_182:
Kolkata, British East India Company (c. 1820 CE)

Architecture & Cityscape:

The Victoria Memorial and Kolkata Race Course are located at the southern end of the Maidan.

Daily Life & Culture:
Kolkata has many buildings adorned with Indo-Islamic and Indo-Saracenic architectural motifs.
Kalighat painting originated in 19th-century Kolkata as a local style that reflected a variety of themes including mythology and quotidian life.
madurai_pandya_kingdom_c_1300_ce:
Madurai, Pandya Kingdom (c. 1300 CE)

Architecture & Cityscape:
Madurai is built around the Koodal Azhagar temple and the Meenakshi Temple, which acted as the geographic and ritual centre of the ancient city of Madurai.

Daily Life & Culture:
Madurai has been an academic centre of learning for Tamil culture, literature, art, music and dance for centuries.
During the third Tamil sangam, the comparative merit of the poets was decided by letting the works float in the lotus tank of the temple.
mahabalipuram_pallava_dynasty_c_700_ce:
Mahabalipuram, Pallava Dynasty (c. 700 CE)

Architecture & Cityscape:
Some of the best examples of Pallava art and architecture are the Vaikuntha Perumal Temple at Kanchipuram, the Shore Temple and the Pancha Rathas of Mahabalipuram.
The best examples of this period are the Shore Temple at Mahabalipuram and the Kanchi Kailasanathar Temple at Kanchipuram both constructed by Narasimhavarman II who was known as Rajasimha.
mohenjo-daro_indus_valley_civilization_c:
Mohenjo-daro, Indus Valley Civilization (c. 2500 BCE)

Architecture & Cityscape:
Based on these fortifications and the structure of other major Indus Valley cities such as Harappa, scholars have postulated that Mohenjo-daro was an administrative center.
Harappa and Mohenjo-daro share a similar architectural layout and were generally not as heavily fortified as other Indus Valley sites.

Daily Life & Culture:
Numerous objects found in excavation include seated and standing figures, copper and stone tools, carved seals, balance-scales and weights, gold and jasper jewellery, and children's toys.
Many bronze and copper pieces, such as figurines and bowls, have been recovered from the site, showing that the inhabitants of Mohenjo-daro understood how to utilize the lost wax technique.

rajgir_mahajanapadas_c_500_bce:
Rajgir, Mahajanapadas (c. 500 BCE)

Architecture & Cityscape:
A Śvētāmbara Jaina temple located at the foothills of Rājagṛha, this temple is also known as "Gāon Mandira" (village temple).
The principal deity of this temple is a black-coloured stone idol of Munisuvrata.

Daily Life & Culture:
Both Mahavira and Buddha taught their beliefs in Rajgir during the 6th and 5th century BC, and the Buddha was offered a forest monastery here by the king Bimbisara.
As such, the city of Rajgir became one of the Buddha's most important preaching locations.

srirangapatna_kingdom_of_mysore_c_1790_c:
Srirangapatna, Kingdom of Mysore (c. 1790 CE)

Architecture & Cityscape:

taxila_maurya_empire_c_250_bce:
Taxila, Maurya Empire (c. 250 BCE)

In about the 1st century BCE or 1st century CE, an Indo-Scythian king named Azilises had three mints, one of which was at Taxila, and struck coins with obverse legends in Greek and Kharoṣṭhī.

Economy & Trade:
Established during the Vedic period, Old Taxila was for a time the capital city of kingdom of Gandhāra.
The earliest settlement at Taxila is found in the Hathial section, which yielded pottery shards that date from as early as the late 2nd millennium BCE to the 6th century BCE.

warangal_kakatiya_dynasty_c_1250_ce:
Warangal, Kakatiya Dynasty (c. 1250 CE)

Political & Security:
Warangal benefits from the Godavari lift irrigation scheme which is designed to lift water from the Godavari river to irrigate drought prone areas in the Telangana region.
The Warangal Fort, Thousand Pillar Temple and the Ramappa Temple are included in the list of World Heritage Sites recognised by UNESCO.

Architecture & Cityscape:
The Kakatiya architecture is more significant in Hanamakonda — their first capital and Warangal being their second capital.
There are hundreds of monuments in the core of Hanamakonda and Warangal of which Thousand Pillar Temple, Ramappa Temple, Ramappa Lake, Warangal Fort and Kota Gullu are prominent.
